title: Links without content
article:
Links without content
2021-02-13/13th February 2021

Markup for hyperlinks looks like this ...

!parseOff
^URL|link description^
!parseOff

... but it can now also look like this ...

!parseOff
^URL^
!parseOff

... which lets you provide hyperlinks without a link description. In those cases the URL is used as the link description, and as the alt text. This was done as a syntactic sugar: you don't always want to describe a link, but under the previous mark up you'd have to post the URL in the link tag twice.

Here's a live example: ^https://www.google.com/^.

title: Image and pre-formatted text support
article:
Image and pre-formatted text support 2021-01-24/24th January 2021 The ^previous article|?20210123^ required me to add image support to the site. This was simple enough: the files go in an assets folder and it's a trivial task to include them in the deploy scripts. Markup looks like this ... !parseOff $URL|alt text$ !parseOff ... and this inserts the following HTML ... !parseOff !parseOff CSS pins it to a maximum width of 75% of its container. The parsing wasn't exactly tricky either. The syntax is very similar to that of hyperlinks - provide URL and link text - and so it follows that the javascript handling and tags is also very similar. But I've _pragmatically_ duplicated it with minor modifications instead of doing the proper refactor job that would enable some code reuse. I can feel the presence of the technical debt here, but as of right now, I'm too lazy to do the honourable thing to tackle it properly. I've been in the product owner game too long: add value, keep the increment releasable. Oh, and this very post here required me to add support for pre-formatted text so I can show the markup being marked up without the parser marking it up. In the article source, if a line contains only the !parseOff string it will disable the parser until it encounters another line containing only the !parseOff string. All the text in between will be inserted into a 
 node.

!parseOff
!!parseOff
This text will appear pre-formatted.
!!parseOff
!parseOff

... which produces the following HTML ...

!parseOff

This text will appear pre-formatted.


!parseOff

New lines are honoured, and double exclamations *!!* get replaced by single *!* for the sole reason that it currently allows me to show a !parseOff directive in the output of a !parseOff directive. You can see this for yourself in the ^source of this article|articles/20210124.txt^.

It might, I acknowledge, play havoc with my later ASCII art.

title: Hacker News CSS
article:
Hacker News CSS
2021-01-23/23rd January 2021

I read ^Hacker News|http://news.ycombinator.com^ as a long-time lurker since I find it to be a useful aggregator of tech-related news. Some of the commentary can also be insightful. I don't have an account and I've never contributed to the community there but it always figures among the _daily read_ bookmarks on most of my devices.

On my Macbook Air it looks tired. Might be the colours; might be the fonts. It's probably as simple as modern design trends leaving it behind. I'm not a graphic designer and so I struggle to articulate why I feel the way I do. However, what I am is someone who knows how to install a browser plug-in and has just enough CSS-fu to hack away at something.

So I made it look like this ...

$assets/20210123-hacker-news-css.png|Hacker News CSS$

You can do so too: the ^stylesheet is on github|https://github.com/davidmcglashan/custom-css/blob/main/hacker-news-y-combinator.css^. It probably looks pony on anything other than a modern Mac running Safari since that's the only platform it's been tested on. But, hey! It's a public repo; send me a pull request.

Creating the CSS was an interesting exercise in hacks-undoing-hacks. The Hacker News main page's layout is driven by nested <table>s and often I needed by use an _!important_ directive in a rule to make it stick. While I was grateful on one hand that such things are possible, on the other hand it felt dirty and wrong. Essentially, I moved the ickiness of the site from the consumer to the developer. It's more pleasant to look at now that more of the eldritch horror has been pushed back under the surface.

title: The Mandalorian: s1e2 - The Child
article:
The Mandalorian: s1e2 - The Child
2021-01-06/6th January 2021

The opening ten minutes feature zero lines of dialogue and yet you're utterly transfixed as the masked and taciturn "Mando" and _too-young-to-talk-and-also-a-puppet_ Child trudge back to the waiting spaceship, shrugging off the obstacles in their way. Plot unravels and characters develop and no-one says a damn thing and it is magical. 

It didn't last. Just as in Pixar's unimpeachably great _Up_, someone has to talk eventually, even after a stunning dialogue-free opening. Fortunately, what remains is largely in _Jawa_ and we're treated to a couple of lengthy and believable action set pieces to fill up most of the remaining running time. 

There are three minute pop songs ^with longer lyrics|https://transcripts.thedealr.net/script.php/the-mandalorian-2019-Y240/s1/e2^.

I was done with _Star Wars_ after two episodes of the sequel trilogy. After two episodes of _The Mandalorian_ I am very much ready for more. It's too rich a universe to be spoiled by clunky movies. I very much believe that _Star Wars_ best on-screen future is telling stories in this form, away from Skywalkers and large spectacle.

I have spoken -- a deliberate last line, right?

title: Short
article:
Short
2020-12-28/28th December 2020

This article has been left deliberately short in order to test how shorter articles are handled by the software that builds this site.

title: A differently specified subset of Markdown
article:
A differently specified subset of Markdown
2020-12-26/26th December 2020

Yes, I've written my own Markdown parser. This is mostly because ...

# I want to write it myself (I can still do code!)
# I don't want to have to depend on any third-party library, no matter how small and efficient it is.
# I only intend to use a handful of HTML tags (<a>, <strong>, <ul>, etc.) so I don't need a complete parser. I will unapologetically use a differently specified subset of Markdown.

Tag support currently runs to ...

<em> tags with _
<strong> tags with *
<ol> done with # as the first char on each line
<ul> done with * as the first char on each line
<a> done with ^ where the link text and the href are separated by a |

title: First
article:
First
2020-12-16/16th December 2020

Go back far enough on any blog and you'll eventually get to the first post. This is mine. It doubles as a rudimentary test of the "markdown" parser, so we can test that it will do things like _emphasis_ and *strong text passages*.

This line has a _ inserted for fun. Hopefully the parser will leave it alone and not render the rest of the article in italics. We should be able to leave stray * in there too ...

_This one_ has *a little* bit of _ everything

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur. Excepteur sint occaecat cupidatat non proident, sunt in culpa qui officia deserunt mollit anim id est laborum.

Ordered lists are also desirtable.

# This will be a list wrapped in <ol> tags.
# And so will this be
# and this one.

And the list is done.
# but here's a new one!

# Can we start a new list after a blank line?
* Can we start an _unordered_ list on the next line? Let's hope so!

Now it's' time to think about how ^hyperlinks|http://www.google.com/^ might work. Or ^hyperlinks with *styled _text_* in them|http://www.cnn.com/^
